content.title: The New Product Launch
content.scenario: Your company has developed a new Class 3B laser engraving system. Before you can sell it in the United States, you need to understand your regulatory obligations.
content.question: What is your FIRST regulatory step?

options.text: Begin manufacturing immediately - compliance can be handled later
Submit a Product Report to FDA before introducing the product into commerce
Wait for FDA pre-approval of the product design
Apply for a variance from all laser safety requirements

feedback.correct: Correct! FDA requires Product Reports to be submitted PRIOR to introduction into commerce. CDRH does not pre-approve products - manufacturers self-certify based on testing programs.
feedback.incorrect: Not quite. Manufacturers must submit Product Reports BEFORE introducing products into commerce. Unlike some medical devices, laser products don't require pre-approval, but they do require pre-market reporting.

regulatory_timeline.title: FDA Laser Regulation Evolution
regulatory_timeline.description: Drag through the timeline to see how FDA laser regulations have evolved over six decades. Click on each milestone for details.

milestones.title: Laser Invented
milestones.full_desc: Theodore Maiman demonstrates the first working ruby laser at Hughes Research Laboratories in California. No one yet imagines the regulatory framework that will eventually be needed.
milestones.significance: The beginning of laser technology - safety concerns emerge within years.

milestones.title: Radiation Control Act
milestones.full_desc: Congress passes the Radiation Control for Health and Safety Act, giving FDA authority to regulate electronic products that emit radiation. This is the legal foundation for all FDA laser oversight.
milestones.key_provision: Authorizes performance standards for electronic products
milestones.significance: Establishes FDA's legal authority over laser products

milestones.title: Laser Standard Established
milestones.full_desc: FDA establishes the first comprehensive laser product performance standards. The regulations create Classes I, II, III, and IV based on hazard potential. The standard becomes effective August 2, 1976.
milestones.significance: The foundation of modern FDA laser regulation

milestones.title: Laser Light Shows
milestones.full_desc: Appendix B added to the Compliance Guide specifically addressing laser light shows and demonstration products. This recognizes the unique needs of the entertainment industry while maintaining safety.
milestones.key_provision: Introduces variance process for Class IIIb/IV demonstration lasers

milestones.title: IEC Harmonization
milestones.full_desc: FDA issues major guidance accepting IEC 60825-1 standards as alternatives to many FDA requirements. This reduces the burden on manufacturers selling products internationally.
milestones.key_provision: Accepts IEC 60825-1 Editions 1.2 and 2 for 17 regulatory sections

milestones.title: Modernization
milestones.full_desc: January 20, 2023 final rule amends records and reporting requirements, removing or reducing some annual reports and test record requirements that were duplicative or unnecessary.

levels.title: The Big Picture
levels.content: FDA regulates laser products under Title 21 of the Code of Federal Regulations (CFR). The regulations are designed to protect the public from unnecessary radiation exposure while allowing beneficial uses of laser technology.

levels.title: Key Regulatory Parts
levels.content: The three main parts governing laser products are: Part 1002 (Reporting), Part 1010 (General Provisions), and Part 1040 (Laser Standards). Each part contains specific requirements for manufacturers.

details.title: Records and Reports
details.focus: What manufacturers must report and when

levels.title: Regulatory Hierarchy
levels.content: Understanding how the pieces fit together: CDRH (Center for Devices and Radiological Health) within FDA enforces the regulations that Congress authorized through the Radiation Control Act.

structure.CDRH: Center for Devices and Radiological Health - enforces regulations
structure.Manufacturers: Must comply with all applicable requirements

interactive_diagram.title: FDA/CDRH Organizational Structure
interactive_diagram.description: Click on each organization to understand its role in laser product regulation.

nodes.label: HHS
nodes.full_name: Department of Health and Human Services
nodes.laser_responsibility: Oversees FDA as part of federal health agencies

nodes.label: FDA
nodes.laser_responsibility: Implements Radiation Control Act; issues regulations under 21 CFR

nodes.label: CDRH
nodes.full_name: Center for Devices and Radiological Health
nodes.laser_responsibility: Direct oversight of laser products; issues Laser Notices; reviews reports

contact.address: 10903 New Hampshire Avenue, Silver Spring, MD 20993

nodes.label: OHT8
nodes.laser_responsibility: Office of Radiological Health - technical expertise on laser products

nodes.label: DSMA
nodes.laser_responsibility: Provides guidance documents and assistance to manufacturers

questions.question: Which FDA Center has direct responsibility for laser product regulation?
questions.explanation: CDRH - Center for Devices and Radiological Health - has direct responsibility for laser products.

questions.question: What is the primary phone number for laser product reporting questions?
questions.explanation: 1-800-638-2041 is the Division of Small Manufacturer's Assistance (DSMA) helpline.

reporting_overview.title: FDA Laser Product Reporting Requirements
reporting_overview.description: Three main types of reports are required for laser products. Understand when each applies.

rows.when: Modifications affecting emission, compliance, or detection
rows.deadline: PRIOR to introduction of modified product

nodes.text: Do you have a new laser product or modification?
Is it a new product or modification?
Is it exempt under Laser Notice 41?
Does change affect emission, compliance, or detection probability?
Submit FORM FDA 3632 Product Report BEFORE introduction
Submit Supplemental Report referencing original accession
Document exemption eligibility. No Product Report required.
Document change. Report in next Annual Report only.
Is it time for Annual Report? (Due Sept 1)
Submit Annual Report for year ending June 30

branching_scenario.title: The Product Launch Challenge
branching_scenario.description: Navigate a real-world compliance scenario as a regulatory affairs manager at a laser company.
branching_scenario.scenario_setup: You are the Regulatory Affairs Manager at LaserTech Industries. Your company is preparing to launch a new Class 3B laser marking system. The product is ready for manufacturing, and sales wants to start taking orders.

scenes.title: The Launch Meeting
scenes.content: The VP of Sales asks when the product can be introduced into commerce. Manufacturing says they're ready to start production. What's your recommendation?

decisions.text: Approve immediate production and sales - we can submit the report later
decisions.feedback: INCORRECT: FDA requires Product Reports to be submitted PRIOR to introduction into commerce. This includes taking orders for delivery.

decisions.text: Submit Product Report first - we cannot introduce until we have an accession number
decisions.feedback: CORRECT: Product Reports must be submitted before introduction into commerce. CDRH will assign an accession number upon receipt.

decisions.text: We need to wait for FDA pre-approval before proceeding
decisions.feedback: PARTIALLY CORRECT: While you do need to submit the report first, FDA does not 'pre-approve' laser products. Manufacturers self-certify based on testing programs.

scenes.title: Six Months Later
scenes.content: Engineering has improved the beam attenuator mechanism. The change affects how operators verify the attenuator is functioning during pre-use checks. What action is required?

decisions.text: No action needed - it's a safety improvement, not a compliance issue
decisions.feedback: INCORRECT: Changes affecting the probability of detecting noncompliance require Supplemental Reports. The modified checking procedure could affect detection.

decisions.text: Submit a Supplemental Report before introducing the modified product
decisions.feedback: CORRECT: Changes that affect detection probability require Supplemental Reports per 21 CFR 1002.11.

decisions.text: Just document the change in the next Annual Report
decisions.feedback: INCORRECT: This change affects compliance detection and requires immediate Supplemental Reporting, not just Annual Report documentation.

scenes.title: Annual Reporting
scenes.content: It's July 1st. What is your reporting obligation?

decisions.text: Submit the Annual Report by September 1
decisions.feedback: CORRECT! Annual Reports are due September 1 for the 12 months ending June 30.

decisions.text: Annual Reports are due January 1 for the calendar year
decisions.feedback: INCORRECT: Annual Reports use a fiscal year ending June 30, with reports due September 1.

scenes.title: FDA Notification
scenes.content: FDA has detected that you introduced products without submitting a Product Report. CDRH has ordered you to cease introduction until compliance is achieved.
